introduction: Automatic License Plate Readers (ALPRs) are smart cameras that scan your car’s license plate to track the places you go. They’re placed in high traffic areas—like parking lots and highways—to collect vast amounts of information about your travel habits.

interventionsParagraph: You can push back against ALPR surveillance by supporting local legislation that limits how long license plate data can be stored and who can access it. Contact your city council to advocate for banning ALPR data sharing with federal agencies like ICE. On a personal level, stay informed about where ALPR cameras are placed in your community and support organizations working to restrict their use. Check if your area uses Flock Safety cameras and use tools like haveibeenflocked.com to see if your data has been collected.

introduction: Facial recognition systems use biometric identifiers such as face geometry, gait, or body movement to identify or assess people in real time. These tools are increasingly paired with behavior analysis or emotion detection systems that claim to identify suspicious activity, intent, or risk.

interventionsParagraph: You can resist facial recognition surveillance by supporting local and state bans on government use of this technology. Many cities have already passed such bans, and your voice can help expand them. When possible, limit your exposure to surveillance cameras in stores and public spaces that use facial recognition. Support retailers and businesses that refuse to deploy this technology. Use privacy-conscious tools and settings on social media to limit the photos available for facial recognition training datasets.

introduction: Social media surveillance systems collect and analyze online content to infer beliefs, emotions, affiliations, or potential risks. This is often described as sentiment analysis or social listening but is frequently used to monitor protest activity, labor organizing, immigration cases, or political dissent. These systems rely heavily on natural language processing and network analysis.

interventionsParagraph: Protect yourself from social media surveillance by reviewing and tightening your privacy settings across all platforms. introduction: Workplace surveillance tools, often called bossware, monitor employee behavior through keystroke logging, screen recording, application tracking, location monitoring, and productivity scoring. Increasingly, these systems use AI to generate performance insights or predict attrition and compliance.
dataCollected: Browsing History,Search History,Emails,Private Chats,Messages,Websites Visited,Keystrokes,Post Sentiment,Web Analytics,Geolocation,Device Information,Device Activity,Device Activity,Screen Activity,Physical Function,Websites Visited,Face
interventionsParagraph: If your employer uses monitoring software, learn your rights under local and state labor laws. Many jurisdictions require employers to disclose monitoring practices. Advocate for workplace transparency policies and support labor organizing efforts that push back against invasive surveillance. On personal devices, keep work and personal activity separate. Support legislation that requires employers to notify workers about monitoring and limits the types of data that can be collected.

introduction: Surveillance advertising relies on real-time bidding systems that auction access to individuals based on detailed behavioral profiles. AI models predict interests, vulnerabilities, and likelihood of response within milliseconds.
interventionsParagraph: Limit your exposure to surveillance advertising by using privacy-focused browsers, ad blockers, and tracker blockers. Opt out of personalized advertising wherever platforms offer it. Review and revoke app permissions that share your location or activity data. Support legislation like comprehensive federal privacy laws that would restrict real-time bidding and the sale of personal data to advertisers and data brokers without meaningful consent.
resourceLinks: https://www.brookings.edu/articles/how-to-reform-police-monitoring-of-social-media/ (How to Reform Police Monitoring of Social Media)

introduction: Predictive policing systems use historical data to forecast crime risk, locations, or individuals.
interventionsParagraph: Push back against predictive policing by attending city council and police oversight meetings where these tools are discussed or funded. Support community organizations that advocate for alternatives to surveillance-based policing. Demand transparency about which predictive tools your local police department uses and how decisions based on them are audited. Support legislation that requires impact assessments before law enforcement can deploy algorithmic tools.

https://sls.eff.org/technologies/predictive-policing (EFF - Further Reading),

https://www.aclu.org/news/criminal-law-reform/predictive-policing-software-more-accurate (ACLU - Further Reading)

introduction: AI systems that analyze medical records, insurance claims, prescription histories, and behavioral data to estimate patient risk. These systems are used to predict outcomes such as hospitalization, treatment adherence, or future cost and to guide decisions about care access, coverage, and resource allocation using risk scoring and pattern analysis.
interventionsParagraph: Protect your health data by carefully reviewing privacy policies for health apps, fitness trackers, and patient portals. Opt out of data sharing wherever possible and ask your healthcare provider how your data is used and who it is shared with. Be cautious about sharing health information on social media or through unencrypted channels. Support legislation that strengthens health data privacy protections beyond HIPAA, especially for reproductive health, mental health, and genetic data.
resourceLinks:
https://www.casact.org/sites/default/files/2021-08/CS19-Risks_and_Ethical_Issues_with_Predictive_Analytics.pdf (ERM - Risks and Ethical Issues with Predictive Analytics and Artificial Intelligence), 
https://www.forbes.com/sites/abigaildubiniecki/2024/11/14/post-roe-your-period-app-data-could-be-used-against-you/ (Forbes - Post-Roe, Your Period App Data Could Be Used Against You)

introduction: Cell site simulators, often called Stingrays, mimic cell towers to force nearby phones to connect, revealing location and identifiers.
interventionsParagraph: Protect yourself from cell site simulators by using encrypted messaging and calling apps like Signal, which protect your communications even if intercepted. Support legislation that requires law enforcement to obtain a warrant before deploying Stingray devices. Stay informed about whether your local police department has purchased this technology through public records requests and freedom of information tools. Advocate for stronger oversight of surveillance equipment purchases.
resourceLinks:
https://sls.eff.org/technologies/cell-site-simulators-imsi-catchers (EFF - Further Reading),  
https://www.aclu.org/issues/privacy-technology/surveillance-technologies/stingray-tracking-devices (ACLU - Further Reading), 
https://theintercept.com/2016/09/12/long-secret-stingray-manuals-detail-how-police-can-spy-on-phones/ (The Intercept - Long-Secret Stingray Manuals Detail How Police Can Spy on Phones)

introduction: Open Source Intelligence systems aggregate publicly available data such as social media posts, public records, images, and location data to build comprehensive profiles of individuals or groups.  AI systems that combine data from multiple sources such as location information, video, transaction records, communications, and online activity to model how a person moves, behaves, and interacts over time. These systems are used to identify routines, relationships, and deviations from normal behavior by continuously aggregating and analyzing longitudinal data.
